topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. I was supposed to be looking for old photo albums, but my fingers kept brushing against something unusual tucked inside a brittle cardboard box. It was a stack of notebooks, pristine, every single one filled with meticulously handwritten assignments from middle school. My mother, who always seemed so organized, had done this? A secret archive of every single thing I’d ever turned in? A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I pulled one out. The ink was still dark, the handwriting familiar, yet suddenly alien. I flipped through pages detailing essays on the periodic table and poetry that felt intensely personal, like someone had been watching my every move. I quickly shoved the books back, my breath catching in my throat. If she kept this, what else does she keep? The shadows in the corners of the attic seemed to deepen, and I started checking the window, half-expecting someone to be watching from the street below.

[story 2]

I found the hidden drawer behind the loose wallpaper in the study. Inside, nestled beneath old tax documents, was a trove. Hundreds of graded papers, every homework sheet, every test booklet from my entire academic career. My hands shook as I lifted a stack. It was overwhelming. Each page felt like a tiny accusation, a documented history of my compliance. I started moving them, shuffling them under the desk, creating a barricade of paper around me. Every time I glanced towards the hallway, my muscles tensed, ready to spring. Why would she hoard this? It wasn't about neatness; it felt like control. I couldn't bring myself to look at any of them for long. The silence of the house suddenly felt heavy, oppressive, as if the very air was waiting for me to make a mistake.

[story 3]

It was a simple thing, a misplaced box in the garage, but when I opened it, the contents stopped me cold. A lifetime of schoolwork. Every report card, every completed project, every assignment submitted over the years. I stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling a sudden, intense need to check the locks, to listen for footsteps outside. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm in the quiet space. It wasn't just the volume of the papers; it was the implication. She wasn't just keeping records; she was cataloging everything. A creeping sense of being observed washed over me. I backed away slowly, my eyes darting toward the garage door, convinced that the act of discovery had somehow alerted whatever was watching.
